title: Dog Urinating When Excited or Submissive: Causes and Solutions
meta_desc: Learn why dogs urinate during greetings or submission. Discover why it's usually not an emergency and how to manage this behavior at home.
content_html:
What is Excited and Submissive Urination?

Excitement urination typically occurs in young puppies who haven't yet gained full control over their bladder sphincters; they simply leak when they become overly stimulated or happy during greetings. Submissive urination is a social behavior where a dog leaks urine to show they are not a threat. This is often accompanied by specific body language like cowering, tucking the tail, or rolling onto their back.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for this issue is Low. In most cases, this is a behavioral or developmental stage rather than a medical emergency. However, if you notice your dog straining to urinate, blood in the urine, or if they are leaking urine while sleeping or resting, you should schedule a non-emergency appointment with your veterinarian to rule out urinary tract infections or incontinence.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Providing your veterinarian with a video of the behavior and a photo of the urine on the floor can be extremely helpful. A video allows the vet to analyze the dog's body language to confirm if the behavior is truly submissive or excitement-based. A photo of the urine helps the vet check for abnormalities in color or clarity, which can help differentiate between a behavioral issue and a medical condition like a UTI.

faqs.question: Will my puppy grow out of excitement urination?
faqs.answer: Yes, many puppies outgrow this behavior by the time they are one year old as they develop better physical bladder control and become more accustomed to social stimuli.

faqs.question: How should I react when my dog has an accident?
faqs.answer: It is important to remain calm and avoid scolding or punishing the dog. Punishment increases anxiety, which often worsens submissive urination.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to this?
faqs.answer: While it can happen to any breed, more sensitive or high-energy breeds may display these behaviors more frequently. Socialization and confidence building can help.
